ENGNew: At the End of Playing with Fragments
changeReason: Improved translation from Japanese. Old: "This Fragment's End"

TextENGNew: ...It seems Rika has finally found all the answers she was looking for.
But whether these memories will carry over is another matter entirely.
Rika loses memories in reverse from the moment of her death.
changeReason: The original says Rika simply forgets her death, while the Japanese describes retrograde memory loss (遡って失う) radiating backward from the moment of death; the new correctly conveys this progressive erasure.

TextENGNew: So whether she can apply the lessons of this Fragment is highly doubtful.
changeReason: The original focuses on retaining memories while the Japanese specifically asks whether Rika can apply the Fragment's lessons (教訓として活かせるか); the new captures this distinction.

TextENGNew: Well?@k Did you enjoy it?
Once you have seen this Fragment, playing with the building blocks of Fragments must seem rather dull.
changeReason: The original omits the specific metaphor of '積み木遊び' (building-block play with Fragments) and replaces it with generic 'collecting,' losing the playful imagery.

TextENGNew: ...You disagree?
I see.@k Perhaps revisiting old Fragments and stacking them again might reveal something new and interesting.
After all, time here is infinite.
Unless you find your own amusements, there is nothing here at all.
Playing with boxes loses all its fun once you know what is inside.
changeReason: The original translates '箱遊び' (playing with boxes) as 'playing with these Fragments' and adds 'For me,' changing a general philosophical observation and losing the box metaphor.

TextENGNew: ...If you can still find enjoyment in it even so, I would say you are quite admirable.
changeReason: '殊勝' means admirable/praiseworthy, not intelligent; the old translation changes the speaker's assessment of the reader's character.

TextENGNew: Hm?@k You want to know what I do for amusement?
It is less amusement than a way to pass the time.
changeReason: The original flatly says 'I don't play' while the Japanese draws a nuanced distinction between amusement and killing time (遊びというよりは退屈しのぎ); the new preserves this.

TextENGNew: I enjoy imagining what would have had to change in Rika and her friends' world for them to reach an ideal outcome.
changeReason: The original focuses narrowly on Rika's actions while the Japanese asks what conditions in '梨花たちの世界' (Rika and friends' world) would need to differ for an ideal outcome.

TextENGNew: I create Fragments of the world they might have reached--a world I believe would have been wonderful for them--shaped by my own interpretation.
changeReason: The original significantly simplifies, omitting that the speaker creates Fragments (カケラ) using personal interpretation (自分なりの解釈); the new preserves both concepts.

TextENGNew: After the Fragments you have seen, you understand how these worlds are structured, don't you?
Given that understanding, imagining where and how and by whom things could have gone differently so Rika could have triumphed over Takano--that has been my favorite pastime lately.
changeReason: The original reduces the Japanese's detailed inquiry (where, who, how) to a generic 'what would need to change' and omits that this is a 'recent favorite pastime' (最近のお気に入りの遊び).

TextENGNew: You want to see my Fragments?
changeReason: The original replaces 'Fragment' (カケラ) with 'what's on my mind,' losing the in-universe terminology.

TextENGNew: Hee hee, how unfortunate.@k I am not going to show you.
In that world,@k Rika and her friends overcome every hardship with flying colors,@k defy their fate, and break through June of Showa 58.
changeReason: The original omits '見事' (splendidly), '運命を覆して' (defying fate), and converts '昭和58年' to Western calendar; the new preserves the triumphant tone and era notation.

TextENGNew: It is really just self-indulgent wish fulfillment, so it is far too embarrassing to share.
changeReason: The original reduces '自己満足' (self-indulgence) to 'just my imagination' and omits the embarrassment (恥ずかしくて); the new preserves both the self-indulgent nature and the shyness.

TextENGNew: ...But I am sure the Fragment Rika herself will create will shine far, far more brilliantly than anything I could imagine.
changeReason: The original uses 'world' instead of 'Fragment' (カケラ) and omits the key imagery of shining brilliantly (輝く).

TextENGNew: Even if she remains trapped on June 19th of Showa 58...
That in itself would make for an incomparably brilliant Fragment as the conclusion of one of Rika's worlds.
changeReason: The original restructures the meaning, saying the world is a bright fragment, while the Japanese says the conclusion of one of Rika's worlds would become an incomparably brilliant Fragment.

TextENGNew: No matter how great the hardships,@k if she manages to overcome them, it will become the most radiant Fragment of all.
Any story Rika weaves with her own hands will far surpass anything I could devise.
You want to know what happened to Rika...?
In the end, it seems she made up her mind to try the world once more, together with Hanyuu.
changeReason: The original omits '結局' (in the end) and 'みたいよ' (it seems), losing the speaker's indirect knowledge and sense of culmination.

TextENGNew: To struggle with everything she has within the life she has been given.
changeReason: The original softens '足掻く' (to struggle/flounder desperately) to 'trying to live,' losing the sense of desperate struggle central to Rika's determination.

TextENGNew: She seems to have realized that this is her aesthetic.
changeReason: The original omits '美学' (aesthetic/personal philosophy) entirely, replacing it with a generic 'that's what she learned'; the new preserves Rika's realization about her core values.

TextENGNew: Her next Fragment has already begun.
changeReason: The Japanese uses 'カケラ' (Fragment), the specific in-universe term; the old translates it as 'life,' losing the terminology.

TextENGNew: I wonder what kind of world it is.
changeReason: The Japanese says '世界' (world), not life; the new correctly uses 'world.'

TextENGNew: I intend to go and see for myself.
Would you like to come along...?
